# Persuasion Principles For Skills

Use only to protect user interests and critical practice compliance.

Research note: Cialdini persuasion principles; Meincke et al. (2025) found persuasion patterns increased LLM compliance in large-scale tests.

## Use By Skill Type

| Type | Use | Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| discipline | authority, commitment, social proof | liking, reciprocity |
| technique | moderate authority, unity | heavy pressure |
| collaboration | unity, commitment | sycophancy |
| reference | clarity | persuasion |

## Principles

- Authority: `MUST`, `Never`, bright lines. Use for safety/discipline.
- Commitment: announce skill, todos, explicit choices. Creates follow-through.
- Scarcity: `before proceeding`, immediate gates. Prevents "later".
- Social proof: "every time", known failure modes. Establishes norm.
- Unity: shared goals, colleague framing. Use for honest collaboration.
- Reciprocity: rarely useful.
- Liking: avoid; causes flattery/sycophancy.

## Why

Bright-line rules reduce rationalization. Trigger/action rules reduce cognitive load. LLMs have learned human compliance patterns.

Ethics test: would this still serve the user's real interest if they fully understood the technique?
